title: "Is the U.S. About to Deploy Ground Troops? What They’re Not Telling You…"Prof. Jiang Xueqin"
full_text: We continue our analysis of the US Iran war. And in this war, there is one critical question that will determine the outcome of this conflict as well as the future of the Middle East. And this question is, will the United States send in ground troops? So, right now, the United States and Israel are primarily focused on an air campaign, striking Iran from a distance with missiles and drones. In the past, we would call this siege warfare. And as long as it remains an air war, the United States can choose to deescalate and withdraw from the region. They would lose this war, but the loss would not be catastrophic. Whereas, if they choose to launch a ground invasion, it would escalate very quickly and they would be trapped in Iran for the next 5 to 10 years. It would be a catastrophe whether or not they win or lose. Because in order to fight a ground war, the United States would have to expand military recruitment dramatically where young men and women would be pressured to join the army and be sent to fight in Iran. So that's the big question for us. Will the United States commit ground forces? Remember this. There's the idea of mission creep where maybe in the beginning you're like, I'll send in 5,000 troops to do a small mission. But then it doesn't go well, so you send in 10,000. It slowly creeps up. It's what we call mission creep. That's how the United States got stuck in Afghanistan and Iraq. All right. So that's the question we're examining today. So in this analysis, I use game theory to make predictions about the future. So right now, I'm going to make my prediction and then I will show you my analysis as to how I make this prediction. Now, what's important to understand is that this is only my assessment based on my analysis. And whether or not it turns out correct will determine the validity of my framework. I don't have inside information. I don't know more than you do. I'm just working from open-source intelligence and doing game theory analysis. So, according to game theory, this is yes, United States will eventually commit ground troops. So, what I'm going to do in this video is explain to you my game theory analysis as to how I come to this conclusion, and we can watch how events unfold. If I'm wrong, then my entire framework needs revision. All right. So, let me explain my analysis. All right. So, to understand what's going on, I need to teach some basic military theory. So, in warfare, in military strategy, there's a concept called escalation dominance. Escalation dominance. And the conventional theory is that whoever has escalation dominance has the decisive advantage. So what this means is let's just say you and I get in a fight. I have a stick, you have a gun. Well, gun is more lethal than a stick. Therefore, you have escalation dominance over me and therefore you should win in theory. and applied to this war in the Middle East. United States and Israel have escalation dominance because they have overwhelming firepower, advanced technology, and nuclear weapons while Iran doesn't. Therefore, the United States and Israel have a massive advantage over Iran. But what I will show you today is that this theory is incomplete. It actually doesn't work the way people think it works. Right? So let's start off with a very simple example to understand how escalation really works. Let's just say that two countries get into a conflict. Okay, country A and country B. And how the escalation process will work is that maybe A and B have a border dispute. And then A says, "Hey, that's our territory." And then B is like, "No, it's not. You need to withdraw." So there's a conflict. And it's usually a very small conflict. Both sides think they have legitimate claims. So they start making threats to each other. So the first step is a dispute. Second step is they start threatening each other, right? We'll defend our interests and then they might start moving troops to the border, right? Mobilize, mobilize. And then they engage in skirmishes, small firefight, small firefight. And then they start a limited war. And then one side launches air strikes and the other retaliates with missile attacks. All right? and eventually you get fullscale war. Right? So there are certain things that we need to keep in mind about this example. First of all, the conflict is not happening in isolation. You cannot look at this situation as just a fight between A and B to see who's militarily stronger. There are other actors involved as well. So for example, there are allies or neighboring countries. There's an international community watching you. This is important because there's also the United Nations, right? Eventually, the international community will intervene and they'll ask people, "Hey, what happened? Who started this?" Right? And also for countries that care about legitimacy, there's also international law, right? If you start a war, you have to justify to the world why you went to war. So, you have to explain your actions. All right? So in other words in this escalation process there's three factors that you have to consider. These three factors are of course national interest, second is military capability and the third is legitimacy or justification and what drives countries up the ladder is pressure. In other words, it is very difficult to skip steps on the escalation ladder. Right? You have to go step by step by step because pressure is building in your system. And the thing to remember about this, and this is really important, is that it's not about how fast you climb up the escalation ladder. If you escalate too quickly and you overreact, then you lose international support, right? What you want to do is climb it strategically. And that means you have to remain measured and deliberate. Because if you remain measured and deliberate, you have three advantages over your opponent. The first is strategic clarity. Second is international legitimacy and the third is operational control. What this means is you know what you're trying to achieve. You have the moral high ground and you are maintaining discipline in your military actions. But in order to have all three, you have to remain controlled. Because remember, it's not just about defeating your opponent. It's about justifying yourself to your allies, to the international community, and to your own population as well. Because you might win the battle, but then you lose all international support for 20 years, in which case you've actually lost strategically. All right. So, the main idea I want you to remember is this, and this is the law of military escalation. Strategic patience is more important than overwhelming force. Strategic patience is more important than overwhelming force. Why? Because patience is about calibration. Calibration means that you time or you structure your military actions in a way that helps you achieve your strategic objectives. You're not just dropping bombs. You're using force in a calculated way that maintains legitimacy, puts pressure on the enemy, and allows you to appear justified among international observers. All right. So, another way of saying this is that calibration is ultimately about strategic flexibility. And the idea of strategic flexibility is in warfare, the side that has the most options, the side that has the most adaptable strategy will usually prevail in the long run. Does that make sense to you guys? All right. So that's the theory. All right. So now what we're going to do is apply this theory to what's happening between the United States and Iran. So, let's analyze why Iran potentially has certain strategic advantages despite being militarily weaker. United States versus Iran. The first major difference is that Iran is strategically active whereas the US is strategically reactive. The dominant power has to maintain credibility. It has to demonstrate that it will respond decisively to challenges. Right? So if Iran provokes, the US has limited choice but to respond or lose deterrence. But Iran can choose when and where to provoke. It can choose the timing of escalation. Therefore, it is active in shaping the conflict. The second big difference is that Iran has strategic clarity. It knows exactly what it wants to accomplish. Remember what Iran wants? force the United States out of the Middle East and establish regional dominance. That's the objective. And it's calibrating its actions systematically to achieve this end goal through a war of attrition that exhausts American political will. The United States, on the other hand, has strategic ambiguity. It wants to defeat Iran. What does that mean exactly, defeat? It could mean regime change. It could mean military degradation. It could mean forcing Iran to abandon its nuclear program. It could mean many things. But because there's so much ambiguity, it creates confusion in military strategy. Right? You can't fight effectively if you don't have a clear political objective. And the last thing is that Iran is strategically flexible and the United States is strategically constrained. So by studying the escalation dynamics, we discover that Iran has certain strategic advantages despite military inferiority. All right. So what does this mean? All right. It means that this conflict will likely become a prolonged war of attrition. But for the United States to prevail in a war of attrition, there have to be fundamental shifts in how it prosecutes the campaign. Right? So remember, to win a sustained conflict, you have to have three things. clear objectives, public support, and resource commitment. All right? You have to know precisely what victory looks like. You have to maintain domestic political will, and you have to commit the necessary resources to achieve your objectives. All right. So, let's now address the central question. Will the United States send in ground troops? All right. The answer based on this analysis is yes. they will eventually have to commit ground forces and it has to do with fundamental realities of how wars are actually won. Basically, when you fight a war of attrition, you have to have the correct force structure pyramid. And the most effective force structure looks like this. Ground forces, infantry, and mechanized units at the bottom. Why? Because they're the most cost effective for sustained operations. What's above them? Armor and artillery, tanks, mobile firepower. They're more expensive to field and maintain. Then you have naval assets. Then you have air power. Does it make sense? This is the force structure pyramid. And the force pyramid represents resource efficiency. How much does it cost to sustain each type of capability? It costs relatively less to deploy infantry, but it costs vastly more to maintain continuous air operations. This is critical because wars of attrition are fundamentally about who can sustain operations longer. Does it make sense, guys? The problem is that the United States has developed an inverted pyramid. Meaning that air power is dominant, then naval power, then armor, then ground forces treated as a last resort. You cannot win a sustained war with this structure. The moment you try to fight a prolonged conflict with this model, you face insurmountable challenges because you cannot sustain the operational tempo. Now, I know this is counterintuitive, but the Gulf War in 1991, the Iraq invasion in 2003, these were not wars of attrition. Okay? because the Americans had such overwhelming advantage and Iraq was so militarily degraded that these were essentially one-sided military operations. Right? These were rapid conventional victories against severely outmatched opponents. But this Iran conflict is a fundamentally different type of war and therefore you cannot fight it with an air centric model. If you want to achieve decisive victory in a sustained conflict, the only viable approach is to return to a realistic force structure pyramid, you don't have strategic alternatives, right? Because your most sustainable capability is ground forces and your least sustainable is continuous air operations. You cannot afford to rely primarily on air power in a prolonged conflict. Eventually, the costbenefit analysis becomes untenable. Does that make sense, guys? All right. So, if the United States wants to win this conflict decisively, then strategically it has no choice but to commit ground troops as the primary instrument of military power. Any questions so far? All right. Now, the question then becomes, will the United States actually deploy ground forces? And this is determined by the game theory strategies of the different actors involved. So, let's analyze the major players. You have United States, you have Israel, you have Saudi Arabia and you have Iran. All right. So, United States wants to neutralize Iran as a regional threat. Iran wants to achieve regional dominance and force US withdrawal. Israel wants to destroy Iran's military capability, but also benefits from a prolonged US commitment to the region. And Saudi Arabia wants Iran weakened but also wants to reduce overall US influence. So by understanding how each actor perceives the game, we can predict behavior. The United States wants to neutralize Iran quickly. But Israel has incentives for this to become prolonged because a quick resolution means America can disengage. Saudi Arabia wants Iran weakened but not permanent US dominance. And Iran wants to impose costs until America gives up. And how do you make America give up? You make the conflict politically unsustainable at home by turning it into a long, costly ground war. Right? Therefore, Iran has every incentive to prolong this conflict. Israel has strategic reasons to see this escalate beyond just air operations. And the United States, despite not wanting a ground war initially, will find itself with diminishing options as air campaigns prove insufficient. The United States doesn't have a sufficiently clear strategy. It's reactive. Therefore, it can be maneuvered into a ground deployment that it didn't initially plan for. So, that's the analysis. Will the United States send in ground troops based on game theory? Yes. The strategic logic of sustained conflict, the force structure requirements, and the incentives of other actors all point toward eventual ground deployment. Whether this prediction proves correct, we will see. But that is what the game theory framework suggests. All right, thank you for watching and let's see how events unfold.
